journal-title-group.journal-title: Indian Journal of Occupational and Environmental Medicine

title-group.article-title: Effectiveness of Personal-Level Protective Measures Against Air Pollution on Respiratory Health: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials

article-meta.aff: College of Nursing, All India Institute of Medical Sciences, Deoghar, Jharkhand, India
1Department of Pharmacology, All India Institute of Medical Sciences, Deoghar, Jharkhand, India
2Department of Biochemistry, All India Institute of Medical Sciences, Deoghar, Jharkhand, India
3Director and CEO, All India Institute of Medical Sciences, Deoghar, Jharkhand, India
4Scientist-C (Information Technology), ICMR-National Institute for Implementation Research on Non-Communicable Diseases, Jodhpur, Rajasthan, India
5College of Nursing, All India Institute of Medical Sciences, Jodhpur, Rajasthan, India
6Department of Pulmonary Medicine, Dr. Ram Manohar Lohia Institute of Medical Sciences, Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, India
7Division of Evidence Synthesis, DMIHER, Wardha, Maharashtra, India

author-notes.corresp: Address for correspondence: Dr. Shiv Kumar Mudgal, College of Nursing, All India Institute of Medical Sciences, Deoghar, Jharkhand, India. E-mail: peehupari05@gmail.com

permissions.copyright-statement: Copyright: © 2026 Indian Journal of Occupational and Environmental Medicine

license.license-p: This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 License (CC BY-NC-ND), where it is permissible to download and share the work provided it is properly cited. The work cannot be changed in any way or used commercially without permission from the journal.

abstract.p: Air pollution, particularly fine particulate matter (PM₂.₅), poses a significant global health risk, contributing to respiratory, and cardiovascular diseases. While personal-level interventions such as air purifiers and respirators are commonly used to reduce exposure, their effectiveness in improving clinical outcomes remains uncertain. This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated the efficacy of such measures in reducing adverse respiratory and cardiovascular outcomes. A systematic search of PubMed, Embase, Scopus, and the Cochrane Library was conducted up to February 2025. Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) assessing the use of air purifiers or respirators in non-occupational settings were included. Meta-analyses were performed using fixed or random-effects models, and effect sizes were expressed as standardized mean differences (SMDs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs). Certainty of evidence was assessed using the Grading of Recommendations, Assessment, Development, and Evaluation (GRADE) approach. Twenty-seven RCTs were included. Personal-level interventions significantly reduced indoor PM₂.₅ (SMD = −2.27; 95% CI: −2.98 to −1.55) and PM₁₀ (SMD = −3.87; 95% CI: −4.59 to −3.16) levels. However, no significant improvements were observed for forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV₁) (SMD = 0.04; 95% CI: −0.07 to 0.15), peak expiratory flow (PEF), forced vital capacity (FVC), respiratory symptoms, or blood pressure. Sensitivity analysis showed a significant reduction in FeNO (SMD = −0.16; 95% CI: −0.28 to −0.04). While effective in reducing particulate matter, personal-level interventions showed limited clinical benefits for respiratory or cardiovascular health.

funding-group.funding-statement: This project is funded by the Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) New Delhi programme Grant for Evidence Synthesis for the Lung Cancer Guidelines: Conducting Systematic Review/Meta-analysis (EM-Del/Centreforguidelines/DHR/2024).

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Ambient air pollution, particularly exposure to particulate matter (PM), has long been established as a major risk factor for human health, contributing significantly to all-cause mortality and cardiopulmonary diseases.[1] Short-term exposure to fine particulate matter with an aerodynamic diameter of less than 2.5 μm (PM2.5) over a period of hours to days is closely linked to increased risks of cardiovascular and respiratory conditions, including myocardial infarction, cerebrovascular disease, heart failure, cardiac arrhythmias, respiratory infections, and the exacerbation of chronic lung diseases.[23]

Globally, ambient air pollution was the seventh largest contributor to disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) and was responsible for approximately 4.2 million premature deaths in 2019. According to estimates from the World Health Organization (WHO), outdoor air pollution accounted for 18% of premature deaths from chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and 23% from acute lower respiratory infections that same year.[45]

Addressing air pollution is critical to public health, as it ranks as the second highest risk factor for noncommunicable diseases. Over recent decades, governmental policies aimed at reducing air pollution have yielded positive public health outcomes. However, implementing interventions at national or community levels, often requires significant time and resources.[678] As a result, individual-level strategies have been proposed to mitigate health risks during the high pollution events. These measures include staying indoors, minimizing outdoor activities, using air filtration systems, and wearing respirators.[910] Personal actions that reduce exposure to air pollution have been shown to help mitigate acute health events, such as asthma exacerbations.[1112]

Despite the growing interest in personal-level interventions, few systematic reviews and meta-analyses have rigorously evaluated their health impacts, and existing evidence remains inconclusive.[137] Therefore, this study aims to collect and analyze data on the efficacy of personal protective measures in reducing the adverse respiratory health effects associated with air pollution.

sec.title: METHODS
sec.p: 2.1. Protocol: This systematic review and meta-analysis were conducted in accordance with PRISMA guidelines (see Supplementary Appendix S1), with the protocol registered in the PROSPERO database (CRD42024553213).

caption.p: PRISMA Checklist

tr.td: Identify the report as a systematic review.
See the PRISMA 2020 for Abstracts checklist.
Describe the rationale for the review in the context of existing knowledge.
Provide an explicit statement of the objective(s) or question(s) the review addresses.
Specify the inclusion and exclusion criteria for the review and how studies were grouped for the syntheses.

Specify all databases, registers, websites, organisations, reference lists and other sources searched or consulted to identify studies. Specify the date when each source was last searched or consulted.
Present the full search strategies for all databases, registers and websites, including any filters and limits used.

Specify the methods used to decide whether a study met the inclusion criteria of the review, including how many reviewers screened each record and each report retrieved, whether they worked independently, and if applicable, details of automation tools used in the process.

Specify the methods used to collect data from reports, including how many reviewers collected data from each report, whether they worked independently, any processes for obtaining or confirming data from study investigators, and if applicable, details of automation tools used in the process.

List and define all outcomes for which data were sought. Specify whether all results that were compatible with each outcome domain in each study were sought (e.g. for all measures, time points, analyses), and if not, the methods used to decide which results to collect.

List and define all other variables for which data were sought (e.g. participant and intervention characteristics, funding sources). Describe any assumptions made about any missing or unclear information.

Specify the methods used to assess risk of bias in the included studies, including details of the tool(s) used, how many reviewers assessed each study and whether they worked independently, and if applicable, details of automation tools used in the process.
Specify for each outcome the effect measure(s) (e.g. risk ratio, mean difference) used in the synthesis or presentation of results.

Describe the processes used to decide which studies were eligible for each synthesis (e.g. tabulating the study intervention characteristics and comparing against the planned groups for each synthesis (item #5)).
Describe any methods required to prepare the data for presentation or synthesis, such as handling of missing summary statistics, or data conversions.
Describe any methods used to tabulate or visually display results of individual studies and syntheses.

Describe any methods used to synthesize results and provide a rationale for the choice(s). If metaanalysis was performed, describe the model(s), method(s) to identify the presence and extent of statistical heterogeneity, and software package(s) used.
Describe any methods used to explore possible causes of heterogeneity among study results (e.g. subgroup analysis, meta-regression).
Describe any sensitivity analyses conducted to assess robustness of the synthesized results.
Describe any methods used to assess risk of bias due to missing results in a synthesis (arising from reporting biases).
Describe any methods used to assess certainty (or confidence) in the body of evidence for an outcome.
Describe the results of the search and selection process, from the number of records identified in the search to the number of studies included in the review, ideally using a flow diagram.
Cite studies that might appear to meet the inclusion criteria, but which were excluded, and explain why they were excluded.
Cite each included study and present its characteristics.
Present assessments of risk of bias for each included study.

For all outcomes, present, for each study: (a) summary statistics for each group (where appropriate) and (b) an effect estimate and its precision (e.g. confidence/credible interval), ideally using structured tables or plots.
For each synthesis, briefly summarise the characteristics and risk of bias among contributing studies.

Present results of all statistical syntheses conducted. If meta-analysis was done, present for each the summary estimate and its precision (e.g. confidence/credible interval) and measures of statistical heterogeneity. If comparing groups, describe the direction of the effect.
Present results of all investigations of possible causes of heterogeneity among study results.
Present results of all sensitivity analyses conducted to assess the robustness of the synthesized results.
Present assessments of risk of bias due to missing results (arising from reporting biases) for each synthesis assessed.
Present assessments of certainty (or confidence) in the body of evidence for each outcome assessed.
Provide a general interpretation of the results in the context of other evidence.
Discuss any limitations of the evidence included in the review.
Discuss any limitations of the review processes used.
Discuss implications of the results for practice, policy, and future research.
Provide registration information for the review, including register name and registration number, or
state that the review was not registered. Indicate where the review protocol can be accessed, or state that a protocol was not prepared.
Describe and explain any amendments to information provided at registration or in the protocol.
Describe sources of financial or non-financial support for the review, and the role of the funders or sponsors in the review.
Declare any competing interests of review authors.
Availability of data, code and other materials

Report which of the following are publicly available and where they can be found: template data collection forms; data extracted from included studies; data used for all analyses; analytic code; any other materials used in the review.

fn.p: From: Page MJ, McKenzie JE, Bossuyt PM, Boutron I, Hoffmann TC, Mulrow CD, et al. The PRISMA 2020 statement: an updated guideline for reporting systematic reviews. BMJ 2021;372:n71. doi: 10.1136/bmj.n71. This work is licensed under CC BY 4.0. To view a copy of this license, visit https:// creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/

sec.p: 2.2 Eligibility criteria: The inclusion and exclusion criteria were developed using the PICO (Population, Intervention, Comparator, Outcome) framework. The population included individuals of any age, gender, or health status exposed to ambient or indoor air pollution, including non-occupationally exposed workers like office staff and taxi drivers, while those with occupational exposure such as agricultural or factory workers were excluded. The intervention focused on personal-level protective measures, such as respiratory protection devices (e.g., masks, respirators) and air filtration devices (e.g., air purifiers), but excluded studies in artificial or industrial environments, including those involving central ventilation systems or stove changes. Comparators included placebo, no intervention, or sham protective measures. The primary outcome assessed was respiratory health, including lung function, symptoms, and biomarkers, while cardiovascular outcomes like blood pressure and heart rate were considered secondary. Studies reporting only cardiovascular or unrelated outcomes were excluded. We included randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and published in English only.

2.2. Study search and selection strategy: We searched PubMed, Embase, Scopus, and the Cochrane Library up to February 28, 2025, using comprehensive search terms related to air pollution, protective devices, and respiratory outcomes. The complete search strategy is detailed in Supplementary Appendix S2. Additionally, we reviewed the reference lists of included studies to identify any relevant articles not captured in the initial search. Three reviewers independently screened the titles and abstracts, and then reviewed full texts of potentially eligible articles. Disagreements were resolved through consensus discussion among the reviewers.

caption.p: Search Strategy

tr.td: Individual exposed to: Air Pollution OR ambient air pollution OR outdoor air pollution OR indoor air pollution OR household pollution OR household air pollution OR particulate matter OR fine particulate matter OR coarse particulate matter OR air pollution OR particulate matter OR particle OR air quality OR PM2.5 OR PM10 OR PM10-2.5 OR PM2.5-10 OR PM coarse

Respiratory Protective Devices OR Air Filters OR respiratory protection device OR face mask OR facemask OR respirator OR air filtration device OR air cleaner OR air filter OR air filtration OR air purifier OR air purification OR air conditioning OR air conditioner OR high efficiency particulate air OR HEPA

Respiratory Tract Diseases OR respiratory OR lung function OR respiratory health effect OR Respiratory System OR respiratory health impact OR respiratory symptom OR cardiopulmonary OR cardiorespiratory OR allergy OR asthma OR chronic obstructive pulmonary disease OR respiratory physiological phenomena OR fractional exhaled nitric oxide OR forced expiratory volume OR forced vital capacity

sec.p: 2.3. Data extraction and process: Data extraction was carried out independently by two reviewers using a pre-specified and standardized data collection form in Microsoft Excel. The extracted information included study identifiers (first author, year, country), participant characteristics (age, sex, BMI, sample size), study design (randomization type, crossover or parallel, blinding, washout periods), details of the intervention (type, equipment, duration), exposure data (pollutant levels during intervention and control phases), and health outcomes of interest. Any discrepancies in data extraction were discussed and resolved by consensus.

2.4. Risk of bias assessment: The risk of bias for each included study was assessed at the outcome level using the Cochrane Risk of Bias 2 (RoB 2) tool. This tool evaluates five domains: bias arising from the randomization process, deviations from intended interventions, missing outcome data, measurement of outcomes, and selection of reported results. For crossover trials, an additional domain for carryover effects was included. Two reviewers independently performed the risk of bias assessments, with a third senior researcher verifying a randomly selected 25% of the included studies for quality control.

2.5 Data synthesis and analysis: Meta-analyses were conducted when ≥3 studies reported the same outcome. Results were pooled as standardized mean differences (SMDs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs). Heterogeneity was assessed using the I² statistic. We decided to apply fixed/random-effects models based on heterogeneity and if there was substantial heterogeneity then we used random effect model. To explore possible sources of heterogeneity, subgroup analysis and sensitivity analysis were conducted. We performed pre-specified subgroup analyses in portable air cleaner studies (air purifier and respirators), Exposure gradient (PM1, PM2.5 and PM10), and study population (children, adults, and elderly).

An assessment of publication bias was conducted through a visual inspection of the symmetry of generated funnel plots, coupled with the application of Egger’s tests to determine statistical significance, as indicated by the associated P value < 0.05. All statistical analyses and graphics were performed in the RevMan, version 5.3.

Evaluation of certainty of evidence: We evaluated the certainty of the evidence using the GRADE (Grading of Recommendations, Assessment, Development, and Evaluation) framework. This approach considers several factors, including risk of bias, inconsistency, indirectness, imprecision, and potential publication bias. Two reviewers independently rated the certainty of evidence for each outcome as high, moderate, low, or very low. Disagreements were resolved with input from a third reviewer. Summary of findings table was generated using GRADE pro GDT software.

sec.title: Characteristics of included studies and participants
sec.p: Figure 1 shows study selection: 3,373 records were retrieved; 610 duplicates were removed, leaving 2,763 titles/abstracts. Screening excluded 2,716, and 47 full texts were reviewed, of which 20 were excluded with specific reasons [see Supplementary Table S1]. Ultimately, 27 RCTs met the inclusion criteria and were incorporated into the systematic review.

caption.p: PRISMA flow chart
List of excluded studies

tr.th: Citation of the study (Vancouver style only)

tr.td: Wang Y, Zhao Y, Xue L, Wu S, Wang B, Li G, et al. Effects of air purification of indoor PM2.5 on the cardiorespiratory biomarkers in young healthy adults. Indoor Air. 2021 Jul;31(4):1125-1133.

Nafees AA, Allana A, Kadir MM, Potts J, Minelli C, Semple S, et al. A cluster randomised controlled trial to reduce respiratory effects of cotton dust exposure among textile workers: the MultiTex RCT study. Eur Respir J. 2024;63(1):2301028.

Allen RW, Carlsten C, Karlen B, Leckie S, van Eeden S, Vedal S, et al. An air filter intervention study of endothelial function among healthy adults in a woodsmoke-impacted community. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2011;183(9):1222-30.

Fong WCG, Kadalayil L, Lowther S, Grevatt S, Potter S, Tidbury T, et al. The efficacy of the Dyson air purifier on asthma control: A single-center, investigator-led, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. Ann Allergy Asthma Immunol. 2023 Feb;130(2):199-205.e2.

Gent JF, Holford TR, Bracken MB, Plano JM, McKay LA, Sorrentino KM, et al. Childhood asthma and household exposures to nitrogen dioxide and fine particles: a triple-crossover randomized intervention trial. J Asthma. 2023;60(4):744-753.
Gherasim A, Jacob A, Schoettel F, Domis N, de Blay F. Efficacy of air cleaners in asthmatics allergic to cat in ALYATEC® environmental exposure chamber. Clin Exp Allergy. 2020;50(2):160-169.

Guan T, Hu S, Han Y, Wang R, Zhu Q, Hu Y, Fan H, Zhu T. The effects of facemasks on airway inflammation and endothelial dysfunction in healthy young adults: a double-blind, randomized, controlled crossover study. Part Fibre Toxicol. 2018;15(1):30.
James C, Bernstein DI, Cox J, Ryan P, Wolfe C, Jandarov R, et al. HEPA filtration improves asthma control in children exposed to traffic-related airborne particles. Indoor Air. 2020;30(2):235-243.

Jhun I, Gaffin JM, Coull BA, Huffaker MF, Petty CR, Sheehan WJ, et al. School Environmental Intervention to Reduce Particulate Pollutant Exposures for Children with Asthma. J Allergy Clin Immunol Pract. 2017;5(1):154-159.e3.

Karottki DG, Spilak M, Frederiksen M, Gunnarsen L, Brauner EV, Kolarik B, et al. An indoor air filtration study in homes of elderly: cardiovascular and respiratory effects of exposure to particulate matter. Environ Health. 2013;12:116.

Skulberg KR, Skyberg K, Kruse K, Eduard W, Levy F, Kongerud J, et al. The effects of intervention with local electrostatic air cleaners on airborne dust and the health of office employees. Indoor Air. 2005 Jun;15(3):152-9.

Sundblad BM, Sahlander K, Ek A, Kumlin M, Olsson M, Larsson K, et al. Effect of respirators equipped with particle or particle-and-gas filters during exposure in a pig confinement building. Scand J Work Environ Health. 2006; 32(2):145-53.
Sublett JL. Effectiveness of air filters and air cleaners in allergic respiratory diseases: a review of the recent literature. Curr Allergy Asthma Rep. 2011 Oct;11(5):395-402.

James C, Bernstein DI, Cox J, Ryan P, Wolfe C, Jandarov R, et al. HEPA filtration improves asthma control in children exposed to traffic-related airborne particles. Indoor Air. 2020 Mar;30(2):235-243.

Yoda Y, Tamura K, Adachi S, Otani N, Nakayama SF, Shima M. Effects of the Use of Air Purifier on Indoor Environment and Respiratory System among Healthy Adults. Int J Environ Res Public Health. 2020 May 23;17(10):3687.

Weichenthal S, Mallach G, Kulka R, Black A, Wheeler A, You H, et al. A randomized double-blind crossover study of indoor air filtration and acute changes in cardiorespiratory health in a First Nations community. Indoor Air. 2013 Jun;23(3):175-84. doi: 10.11n/ina.12019. Epub 2013 Jan 19. PMID: 23210563.

Peng RD, Butz AM, Hackstadt AJ, Williams DL, Diette GB, Breysse PN, et al. Estimating the health benefit of reducing indoor air pollution in a randomized environmental intervention. J R Stat Soc Ser A Stat Soc. 2015 Feb;178(2):425-443.

Niu W, Wang W, Huang C, Zhang Z, Ma L, Li R, et al. Cardiopulmonary benefits of respirator intervention against near road ambient particulate matters in healthy young adults: A randomized, blinded, crossover, multi-city study. Chemosphere. 2022;308(Pt 3):136437.
Shi J, Lin Z, Chen R, Wang C, Yang C, Cai J, et al. Cardiovascular benefits of wearing particulate-filtering respirators: a randomized crossover trial. Environ Health Perspect. 2017;125(2):207-13.

Butz AM, Matsui EC, Breysse P, Curtin-Brosnan J, Eggleston P, Diette G, et al. A randomized trial of air cleaners and a health coach to improve indoor air quality for inner-city children with asthma and secondhand smoke exposure. Arch Pediatr Adolesc Med. 2011;165(8):741-8.

sec.p: The 27 RCTs [Table 1] were primarily conducted in China (n = 15), the UK (n = 5), and the USA (n = 4). Study designs included crossover (n = 14) and parallel-group (n = 13) trials, with 15 double-blinded. Populations comprised healthy individuals (children/adults/elderly; n = 12), asthmatics (n = 10), allergic rhinitis patients (n = 3), and COPD patients (n = 2). Interventions included high-efficiency particulate air (HEPA) air purifiers (n = 14), ionizers (n = 3), laminar airflow devices (n = 2), respirators/facemasks (n = 3), and negative ion generators (n = 2). Comparators were sham devices (n = 20) or no intervention (n = 5). Primary pollutants targeted were PM₂.₅ (n = 16) and allergens (e.g., house dust mite; n = 4). Intervention durations ranged from 2 hours to 12 months. Outcomes included lung function (FEV₁: n = 19; PEF: n = 15; FVC: n = 10), airway inflammation (FeNO: n = 19), respiratory symptoms (n = 10), and pollutant levels (PM₁/PM₂.₅/PM₁₀: n = 11).

caption.p: Characteristics of the studies included

sec.title: Lung function parameters
sec.p: The pooled meta-analyses did not show statistically significant improvements in lung function parameters. For FEV₁, the standardized mean difference (SMD) was 0.04 (95% CI: −0.07, 0.15) with no observed heterogeneity (I² = 0%). Similarly, there were no significant changes in peak expiratory flow (PEF) (SMD = 0.00; 95% CI: –0.13, 0.13) and forced vital capacity (FVC) (SMD = 0.00; 95% CI: –0.14, 0.14), both with I² = 0%. The pooled estimate for FeNO was also not statistically significant (SMD = −0.07; 95% CI: −0.19, 0.04), although substantial heterogeneity was observed (I² = 81%). Sensitivity analysis excluding one outlier study (Drieling et al., 2022)[18] resulted in a statistically significant reduction in FeNO (SMD = –0.16; 95% CI: −0.28, −0.04), with no heterogeneity (I² = 0%) [Supplementary Figure S1]. Subgroup analyses stratified by age group (children, adults, and elderly) and intervention type (air filters vs. face masks) did not yield significant effects for any lung function measure [Supplementary Figures S2–8]. However, subgroup analysis based on pollutant exposure gradients revealed a significant reduction in FeNO (SMD = −0.24; 95% CI: −0.34, −0.15; P < 0.00001), indicating potential benefit in high-exposure scenarios [Supplementary Figures S9–12].

sec.title: Respiratory symptoms severity score
sec.p: Respiratory symptom severity was assessed in ten studies. The pooled estimate did not demonstrate a statistically significant improvement (SMD = −0.18; 95% CI: −0.45, 0.09), with considerable heterogeneity across studies (I² = 60%) [Figure 2]. Sensitivity analysis excluding the study byWarner et al. (1993)[36] reduced heterogeneity (I² = 51%) and modestly shifted the effect estimate (SMD = −0.14; 95% CI: −0.38, 0.09), although the result remained non-significant [Supplementary Figure S13]. Subgroup analysis by age group and gradient of pollutant exposure also did not reveal any meaningful difference in symptom outcomes [Supplementary Figures S14 and S15].

caption.p: Forest plot for respiratory symptoms severity score

sec.title: Level of gradient of exposure
sec.p: Personal-level interventions significantly reduced indoor levels of particulate matter. For PM₁.₀ (MD = −18.27; 95% CI: −21.50, −15.04; I² = 63%). Similar significant reductions were observed for PM₂.₅, the pooled MD was −24.49 (95% CI: −32.06, −16.93), though heterogeneity was high (I² = 97%) and PM₁₀ (MD = −38.14; 95% CI: −51.56, −24.73; I² = 89%). The reduction in ozone levels was not statistically significant (MD = −0.66; 95% CI: −2.52, 1.20; I² = 58%) [Figure 3]. Sensitivity analyses showed that exclusion of one high-variance study (Yang et al., 2022)[37] led to improved precision for PM₁.₀, with the updated MD of −19.86 (95% CI: −21.46, −18.26) and no heterogeneity (I² = 0%). Results for PM₂.₅ and PM₁₀ remained same across sensitivity analyses.

caption.p: Forest plot for level of gradient exposure

sec.title: Cardiovascular outcomes
sec.p: Cardiovascular outcomes were reported in eight studies. There were no significant changes observed in systolic or diastolic blood pressure. The pooled SMD for systolic blood pressure was –0.00 (95% CI: −0.16, 0.16) and for diastolic blood pressure was 0.04 (95% CI: −0.12, 0.20), both with I² = 0% [Supplementary Figure S16]. Subgroup analyses based on age group and exposure gradient levels yielded no statistically significant effects [Supplementary Figures S17–20].

sec.title: Publication bias
sec.p: Funnel plot inspection indicated no notable risk of publication bias for respiratory or cardiovascular outcomes [Supplementary Figures S21–28].

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment
sec.p: Supplementary Figures S29 and S30 present the risk of bias assessments for all included crossover and parallel-group studies, respectively, using the RoB 2.0 tool. All crossover studies were rated as having “some concerns,” primarily due to insufficient reporting of the randomization process and the absence of protocol registration, which limited assessment of selection and reporting biases. Among the parallel-design trials, only three studies—Boyle et al., 2012,[14] Kapoor et al., 2019,[24] and Hansel et al., 2022[21]—were evaluated as having a low overall risk of bias. The remaining parallel trials were rated as having “some concerns,” largely owing to unclear methodological details, especially related to randomization and blinding.

sec.title: Certainty of evidence assessment
sec.p: Supplementary Tables 2 and 3 summarize the rationale for downgrading the certainty of evidence according to the GRADE framework, detailing the evaluation for each outcome–intervention pair included in the review. As all included studies were randomized controlled trials, the initial certainty of evidence was rated as high. However, for most respiratory health outcomes, the certainty was downgraded to low or very low, primarily due to concerns related to indirectness (use of surrogate outcomes), inconsistency (heterogeneity across studies), and imprecision (wide confidence intervals and small sample sizes). Similarly, for cardiovascular outcomes, the certainty of evidence was consistently rated as low, driven using surrogate endpoints such as blood pressure and limited statistical power.

caption.p: Summary of findings

tr.td: SMD 0.04 SD higher (0.07 lower to 0.15 higher)
SMD 0.07 SD lower (0.19 lower to 0.04 higher)
SMD 0.11 SD lower (0.47 lower to 0.25 higher)

fn.p: *The risk in the intervention group (and its 95% confidence interval) is based on the assumed risk in the comparison group and the relative effect of the intervention (and its 95% CI).CI: confidence interval; SMD: standardised mean difference.
GRADE Working Group grades of evidence.
High certainty: we are very confident that the true effect lies close to that of the estimate of the effect.

Moderate certainty: we are moderately confident in the effect estimate: the true effect is likely to be close to the estimate of the effect, but there is a possibility that it is substantially different.
Low certainty: our confidence in the effect estimate is limited: the true effect may be substantially different from the estimate of the effect.
Very low certainty: we have very little confidence in the effect estimate: the true effect is likely to be substantially different from the estimate of effect

caption.p: GRADE Assessment

tr.th: Risk difference with Air filter/respirator devices

tr.td: SMD 0.04 SD higher (0.07 lower to 0.15 higher)
SMD 0.07 SD lower (0.19 lower to 0.04 higher)
SMD 0.11 SD lower (0.47 lower to 0.25 higher)

fn.p: CI: confidence interval; SMD: standardized mean difference Explanations, a. Most studies showed overlapping confidence intervals with an I2 value of 0%, indicating no significant statistical heterogeneity. No serious inconsistency, do not downgrade, b. Desired outcome (respiratory symptoms) was measured by surrogate outcomes (Pulmonary function), the use of a surrogate outcome may not reliably predict clinically important benefits or harms, indirectness was rated as serious, c. The CI cross the clinical decision threshold between recommending and not recommending intervention and an optimal information size was not met. This may lead inconsistency, downgrade by one. d. Variance of point estimates across studies and heterogeneity may be considerable (75%-100%). Inconsistency was serious, downgrade by one. e. No difference between desired and measured outcomes. Do not downgrade, f. Most studies had same direction and no much variance of point estimation. Substantial heterogeneity F value of (50%-90%), indicating significant statistical heterogeneity, serious inconsistency, downgrade by one. g. Desired outcome (respiratory symptoms) was measured by surrogate outcomes (particulate matter). The use of a surrogate outcome may not reliably predict clinically important benefits or harms, indirectness was rated as serious, downgrade by one. h. The CI does not cross the clinical decision threshold between recommending and not recommending intervention but an optimal information size was not met. Potentially limitations are unlikely, do not downgrade, i. Desired outcome (cardiovascular health) was measured by surrogate outcomes (blood pressure). The use of a surrogate outcome may not reliably predict clinically important benefits or harms, indirectness was rated as serious, downgrade by one

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis comprehensively evaluated the efficacy of personal-level protective measures—namely air purifiers and respirators—in mitigating the adverse respiratory and cardiovascular health effects associated with air pollution exposure. While the interventions consistently reduced indoor levels of particulate matter, their clinical impact on respiratory function and cardiovascular health remained limited and inconsistent.

The pooled analysis revealed no statistically significant improvement in core pulmonary function metrics, including forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV₁), PEF, or FVC. This aligns with previous research that demonstrates although portable air filtration devices can effectively reduce particulate concentrations indoors, their ability to produce clinically meaningful changes in lung function parameters is often minimal over short study durations or in relatively healthy populations.[37] Several factors may account for these findings: the interventions’ short duration, limited pollutant exposure reduction in some settings, and a participant pool largely consisting of individuals without significant preexisting respiratory conditions. Further, lung function parameters assess the functional capacity of the lungs that are affected by the extent of restrictive and obstructive damages were conducted to the lungs due to the underlying diseases. These damages are predominantly irreversible except in case of acute bronchial asthma. Therefore, the reduction in exposure to particulate matter does not bring about changes in a short period.

Fractional exhaled nitric oxide (FeNO), a well-established non-invasive biomarker for eosinophilic airway inflammation, did not show significant improvement in the overall analysis. However, a statistically significant reduction emerged in sensitivity analyses excluding one outlier study, and in subgroup analyses focusing on higher exposure gradients. This pattern suggests a possible dose-response effect, wherein personal-level interventions may be more effective when baseline pollutant levels are elevated.[11] Nonetheless, the utility of FeNO as a reliable marker in intervention studies remains uncertain due to its susceptibility to external confounders such as dietary nitrate intake, recent infections, and medication use.[12]

Despite the reduction in indoor air pollution levels, improvements in subjective respiratory symptoms (e.g., cough, wheeze, dyspnea) were not significant across studies. This could be attributed to heterogeneity in symptom measurement tools, placebo effects, or the inherently subjective nature of symptom perception, which often lacks the sensitivity to detect subtle changes over short periods.[1640] Moreover, the intensity and duration of the interventions may have been insufficient to induce measurable improvements in symptom scores, especially in participants with well-controlled baseline conditions.

A key strength of the included trials was the robust and significant reduction in airborne particulate matter—specifically PM₂.₅, PM₁.₀, and PM₁₀—achieved by high-efficiency particulate air (HEPA) filters and respirators. These findings are consistent with prior studies demonstrating the technical efficacy of air purification technologies in reducing particulate concentrations in both residential and controlled environments.[131516] Nevertheless, the absence of corresponding improvements in clinical outcomes underscores the complexity of the relationship between pollutant exposure and health effects. Several possible explanations include short intervention durations, the presence of unmeasured co-pollutants (e.g., volatile organic compounds, nitrogen dioxide), and latency in the manifestation of pollutant-induced health effects.

Regarding cardiovascular outcomes, no significant changes were observed in systolic or diastolic blood pressure. These findings mirror previous evidence indicating that short-term interventions targeting indoor air quality may not exert sufficient influence on vascular tone or autonomic regulation, particularly in relatively healthy individuals or populations with modest baseline cardiovascular risk.[21] Larger, long-term trials focused on high-risk populations (e.g., elderly with hypertension or preexisting cardiovascular disease) are needed to clarify whether these interventions can yield measurable cardiovascular benefits.

Another consideration is the potential variability in individual susceptibility to air pollution and the biological response to exposure. Vulnerable populations—such as children, the elderly, and those with preexisting conditions—may respond differently to interventions. This heterogeneity was partly addressed through subgroup analyses in the present review, but broader exploration is warranted. Prior research has highlighted that health inequalities may arise from differential exposure and differential susceptibility, reinforcing the need for stratified approaches to intervention deployment.[10]

sec.title: Strengths and limitations
sec.p: This review has several strengths. All included studies were randomized controlled trials, ensuring strong internal validity. Methodological rigor was upheld through comprehensive searches, duplicate screening and data extraction, GRADE assessment, and subgroup and sensitivity analyses. However, limitations remain. Many studies had small sample sizes, short durations, and relied on surrogate outcomes like FeNO or blood pressure, limiting insights into long-term effects. Considerable heterogeneity in study design, populations, interventions, and outcome measures also affected consistency. Only English-language studies were included, introducing potential language bias. Moreover, real-world effectiveness may vary due to differences in device quality, adherence, and environmental conditions.

sec.title: Implications for practice and future research
sec.p: Although personal-level protective measures such as HEPA purifiers and respirators showed limited clinical benefit, they effectively reduced indoor air pollutant levels, supporting their use as short-term protective measures—particularly during high-pollution events or for vulnerable groups such as the asthmatics and elderly. However, these should complement—not replace—broader policy efforts targeting pollution sources. Public health strategies must integrate personal protection with regulatory reforms and public education.

Future research should focus on large, long-term RCTs using clinically meaningful outcomes such as exacerbation rates, hospitalizations, or quality of life. Studies should also evaluate long-term exposure, co-pollutants, and social determinants of health. Incorporating wearable sensors and real-time symptom tracking could improve outcome precision. Additionally, economic analyses are required to assess the cost-effectiveness of these personal-level protective measures relative to broader policy approaches.

sec.title: CONCLUSION
sec.p: In conclusion, personal-level protective interventions significantly reduce indoor air pollutant levels, but evidence for meaningful respiratory or cardiovascular health benefits remains limited and uncertain. These findings emphasize the importance of systemic, policy-level interventions to reduce air pollution exposure at the population level, while reserving personal-level measures for supplementary or emergency use.

sec.title: Ethics approval
sec.p: As this is a systematic review and meta-analysis and based on published data, no approval was needed.

sec.title: Conflicts of interest
sec.p: There are no conflicts of interest.

sec.p: a) PubMed

sec.p: b) EMBASE

sec.p: d) Cochrane Central

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: We thank the Guideline Development Group of DHR, ICMR, New Delhi for their expert input in completion of this project.

element-citation.article-title: Effectiveness of filtering or decontaminating air to reduce or prevent respiratory infections: A systematic review
Fine particulate air pollution and daily mortality. A nationwide analysis in 272 Chinese Cities

element-citation.article-title: The effect of using personal-level indoor air cleaners and respirators on biomarkers of cardiorespiratory health: A systematic review

Health benefits of air pollution reduction
Can individual protective measures safeguard cardiopulmonary health from air pollution? A systematic review and meta-analysis

Ambient air pollution and clinical implications for susceptible populations
Personal-level protective actions against particulate matter air pollution exposure: A scientific statement from the American Heart Association

Cardiopulmonary benefits of reducing indoor particles of outdoor origin: A randomized, double-blind crossover trial of air purifiers
Association between bedroom particulate matter filtration and changes in airway pathophysiology in children with asthma
Different cardiorespiratory effects of indoor air pollution intervention with ionization air purifier: Findings from a randomized, double-blind crossover study among school children in Beijing
Randomized trial of a portable HEPA air cleaner intervention to reduce asthma morbidity among Latino children in an agricultural community
Reducing particulates in indoor air can improve the circulation and cardiorespiratory health of old people: A randomized, double-blind crossover trial of air filtration
A randomized, double-blind, crossover intervention study of traffic-related air pollution and airway inflammation in healthy adults
Randomized clinical trial of air cleaners to improve indoor air quality and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease health: Results of the CLEAN AIR study
element-citation.source: Am J Respir Crit Care Med
